All procedures followed the ethical standards of the 1964 Helsinki Declaration and its later amendments.

STABILITY IN INFANT RULE-LEARNING 2 Abstract We tested the stability of infants’ rule learning over a six-months interval in a subsample of N = 86 infants from ManyBabies3 (Visser et al., 2025). Infants completed a gaze-contingent eye-tracking adaptation of the ABA/ABB syllable-triplet task (Marcus et al., 1999) at two time points (T1: 5–12 months; T2: 11–18 months). Rule learning was measured as a novelty preference (greater looking times in rule-inconsistent than rule-consistent trials). Bayesian analyses provided no evidence of rule learning at either time point and only very weak evidence for longitudinal stability. Keywords: MB3, rule learning, eye tracking, stability, language, cognition

STABILITY IN INFANT RULE-LEARNING 3 Brief Report: Stability in Infant Rule-Learning? Infants’ ability to generalise simple repetition-based relations (e.g., ABA/ABB; rule learning) has been tested most prominently with the syllable-triplet paradigm (Marcus et al., 1999). However, a meta-analytic review suggests that evidence for robust generalisation is mixed across studies and methodologies (Rabagliati et al., 2019). In line with this, a close, multi-centre replication found no reliable preference for rule-inconsistent test items (Geambasu et al., 2023), raising questions about the , robustness of the original effect. Rule learning has been discussed as a mechanism supporting later language development, and early performance has been treated as a potentially predictive individual-differences measure (Bettoni et al., 2020). Such interpretations assume that behavioural responses in infant rule-learning tasks are sufficiently stable to support developmental prediction. From a developmental perspective, however, one key question is whether performance begins to stabilise over time rather than being fully stable early in life. This focus on emerging stabilisation is important because several infant cognitive measures, including attention and information processing, undergo rapid change in the first years and often show only limited longitudinal stability (Hendry et al., 2019). A similarly weak stability in ABA/ABB task performance would suggest that infants’ performance is strongly influenced by transient factors such as attention, arousal, or task context. This would challenge its use as a trait-like individual-differences marker. Conversely, evidence for stabilisation would support stronger claims about developmental continuity. More generally, methodological work cautions that infant preference measures often show modest reliability and limited test-retest stability, even when they can detect group-level effects (Byers-Heinlein et al., 2022; Cristia et al., 2016). The present study directly tests longitudinal stability and possible emerging stabilisation in infants’ performance on a gaze-contingent eye-tracking adaptation of the ABA/ABB triplet paradigm (adapted from Marcus et al., 1999). We operationalise rule learning as a novelty preference at test, that is, looking more to rule-inconsistent than

STABILITY IN INFANT RULE-LEARNING 4 rule-consistent trials. Infants were tested twice, approximately six months apart (Measurement Time Point 1 (T1): 5-12 months; T2: 11-18 months). We ask (1) whether infants show a novelty preference at either time point and (2) whether individual differences in this preference show stability or emerging stabilisation across the interval. The T1 data were collected as part of a broader multi-lab project (ManyBabies3; Visser et al., 2025). Please note that the research questions and hypotheses of the present project were pre-registered before the data of the ManyBabies3 project were analysed. Because ManyBabies3 found no evidence for rule learning in 5- to 12-month-olds, our hypotheses may have been formulated differently if the results were already known. Methods We report how we determined our sample size, all data exclusions (if any), all manipulations, and all measures in the study. We preregistered the study (https://doi.org/10.17605/OSF.IO/U2QKR) and made the data collected and R codes available on the Open Science Framework (OSF, https://doi.org/10.17605/OSF.IO/EN43F). Participants As part of ManyBabies3 (Visser et al., 2025), we collected data of N = 86 5- to 11-month-old children (at T1). For the present study, the same children were invited again to the lab 6 months (+/- 2 weeks) later. Most families (52.63%) had an income equal to or higher than the median household income of couples with children in Switzerland (CHF 12’614,-). Caregivers were well educated, and 76.47% had a Bachelor’s degree or higher. Samples differed between measurement time points due to children fussing out during the familiarisation trial or calibration failure (T1: n = 11, M(SD) = 250.64(63.66), 3 girls, T2: n = 5, M(SD) = 487.20(80.89), 4 girls) or age age their caregivers declining to participant at T2 (n = 7, M(SD) = 235.00(63.89) at T1, age 1 girl). For a description of the final sample, see Table 1.

STABILITY IN INFANT RULE-LEARNING 5 Table 1 Final Sample Age (Days) n M(SD) Range % female Measurement Time Point 1 (T1) 74 260.62(59.44) [155, 364] 51.70 Measurement Time Point 2 (T2) 74 444.95(58.11) [337, 546] 51.35 Procedure Data was collected from March to June 2023 for T1 and from September to December 2023 for T2. Participants were recruited from the research unit’s participant database (via birth records) after caregivers registered interest in participation. Infants received a small gift (approximately CHF 5) and a participation certificate. The study was approved by the local ethics committee (hidden for review purposes) and conducted in accordance with Declaration of Helsinki. Both appointments began in the lab’s playroom, where caregivers were briefed on the study procedures and signed informed consent (only at T1). After a warm-up period, families relocated to the study room for the rule-learning task, presented on a 17-inch monitor (800 x 600 px) using an EyeLink 1000Plus eye tracker (SR Research, 500 Hz sample rate). Infants, seated approximately 55 cm from the screen on their caregiver’s lap, viewed full-screen stimuli (34 x 28° visual degrees). A three-point calibration with an animated target was conducted without re-calibration, consistent with the ManyBabies3 procedure. After the rule-learning task, families also completed a caregiver-infant interaction task and caregiver questionnaires (on vocabulary, temperament, attunement beliefs), which were not analysed here. Measures Infants’ rule learning was measured with the ManyBabies3 adaptation of the Marcus et al. (1999) task (for details on task and task construction, see Visser et al., 2025). In this task, infants were presented with a coloured checkerboard. They were familiarised with one of two auditory sequences of 16-syllable triplets following an ABB

STABILITY IN INFANT RULE-LEARNING 6 or ABA pattern. After this two-minute familiarisation phase, 12 test trials were performed. In these gaze-contingent trials, infants were presented with new syllable triplets that followed the pattern presented in the familiarisation phase (consistent trials) or not (inconsistent trials). The trials were terminated once the infants looked away for two seconds continuously. Rule learning was indicated via infants’ looking time, that is, the sum of their fixations’ duration per test trial. From this study, we calculated a proportional score of the log-transformed mean looking time to inconsistent trials per mean sum of log-transformed looking time to consistent and inconsistent trials per infant (i.e., novelty preference). Analyses Plan We pre-processed the eye-tracking data using the Data Viewer (SR Research) software using the default parameters. We excluded trials in which infants were too fussy, caregivers interfered (e.g., pointed to the screen), or the eye tracker could not measure their gaze (T1: 6.00%, T2: 1.28% of all trials). Following the ManyBabies3 analysis guidelines, we analysed trials in which children looked at the screen for at least 1 second. Furthermore, children needed to have at least two valid test trials, one from each trial type. We deviate from the pre-registered analyses in three ways. First, we only report the results on (a) evidence for a novelty preference at each time point and (b) longitudinal stability; the two additional preregistered questions were not pursued because they presupposed a robust rule-learning effect, which was not observed in the overall project (Visser et al., 2025). Second, we tested evidence for rule learning at each measurement time point in our sample with a linear regression against an intercept of 0.5. Third, we implemented the models in a Bayesian framework to accommodate the longitudinal structure and missing values in a modest sample. We tested our research questions with Bayesian Generalised Linear Models (‘Gaussian’ family). For rule learning, we estimated novelty preference at each time point relative to 0.5 (values > 0.5 indicate longer looking at inconsistent trials).
